sentences.text: By then, the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the people didn't go anymore to the factory of Oskar Schindler.
Because by then, Amon Goth didn't let out any Jews anymore from the camp of Plaszow.
Yes.
They were--
Yes, controlled by the SS and the Jewish police.
They were going to the factory.
And for the evening, they were coming back to camp.
They were counting them one by one.
And they went to their barracks.
And each day was going on the same thing.
Why?
He-- he was trying to befriend himself with Amon Goth, in order to get the cheap labor.
Because nothing could be cheaper than to have the Jewish labor, because nobody was getting paid anything.
But he was bribing each and every SS man what he was getting in contact, including Amon Goth, the commandant of Plaszow.
He was bribing him with cognacs-- what was very hard to get at that time-- with cigarettes, with money, and most of the time, with all kinds of woman what he had to-- had to his disposition.
This I find out later on.
After the war, I was searching for a long time, to find out more and more about my past.
With my brother, Roman.
Till today, I don't know.
But I was thinking many times-- because wherever and whoever you will ask, I didn't have the money.
There were two people who were making the list, the payments list of Oskar Schindler.
One was Itzhak Stern, what is portrayed by Ben Kingsley in the movie.
And the other one was a famous name in Plaszow, in ghetto and Plaszow.
His name was Marcel Goldberg.
After the war, he survived in Brunnlitz with-- on Schindler's list, but he was scared to move around Europe or go back to Poland.
He moved to South America, when he died around six or eight years ago.
Those two people were responsible for the list.
No.
Till today, we don't know how.

The only thing-- when I got the list after the war, what is in my possession-- on this list, I was checking my name, my number-- as a prisoner number-- the date of my birth, and the work what I was doing.
Now I see I was that time figure as a metalworker or closer, as an auto body mechanic.
This is maybe the reason why I got on the list with my brother.
We were rejoicing, we were hugging each other with my brother.
Because we knew this is the first time where we had a very slim chance even to think about survival.
Because everybody knew Schindler is very sympathetic to the Jews.
And he will try to do his utmost, in order to keep them alive.
But we have to understand one thing.
That time, we didn't think that we maybe even came through our mind.
But the change started already in 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In certain way, I have to go back.
When General von Paulus surrender with his army under Stalingrad, with 316,000, with the Sixth Elite Army-- this was the best German army.
When he surrendered that time, the turn-- you could see already as the Germans are getting panicky.
This didn't stop absolutely the killing, vice versa, increased the killing of the Jews.
Because during 1933-- '43 and '44, more Jews were killed by the German than ever before.
But this was practically the change what we noticed that time.
No.
We didn't get any news.
The only thing was somebody said something but never proven anything.
Just before we were sent out.
This happened when we left Plaszow was the 15 of October, 1944.
From the camp of Plaszow, they packed us in-- into-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cars, the cattle cars.
And we were sent out.
We were hoping we going that time to Brunnlitz.
But our disappointment was when we arrived to a terrible concentration camp.
The name is-- was Gross-Rosen.
Oh, not a long time, because Gross-Rosen is located the Sudetenland, what is not far from Krakow.
I don't know how many.
Was it a day or day and a half, but not more than that.
Maybe two days.
Very little.
But as long we were there, we knew we have a chance to survive.
And if you have even the smallest feeling of survival, builds you up.
You can go much longer without food, without water, as long you have-- think you can go through those days.
Was a terrible moment.
Because the moment we arrived-- like I said, they were cattle wagons.
The SS men came up.
They were starting to beat us up.
And as-- herding us all to one place.
When we came, we were standing on a big [GERMAN] for a number of hours.
Finally, they made a decision.
And they said, each and every one of you have to line up for showers and changing of the clothing.
That's what we did.
But we got terribly scared.
Because when they were herding us, we saw a tremendous two big chimneys, spewing all kinds of smoke.
And the smell we recognized, because we knew from Plaszow.
They were a smell of burning human bodies.
Because has a sweet smell, and you feel it on your-- end of your tongue.
And we got terribly scared because now we didn't know what is happening to us.
Or we are going to be gassed and destroyed.
Each and every one was shaking.
They ask us to undress.
We did.
And we walked in.
I remember like today.
My brother took me around.
He said, don't worry.
We'll survive.
But I didn't believe, because I saw the end of it.
We still hug each other.
And we went to a very big shower room.
What hit me, the shower heads were approximately 6 inches diameter.
But normally, a shower head has 2 and 1/2 to 3 inches diameter.
And we got terribly scared again.
And they closed the door.
And we were standing maybe for 15 minutes, each and every one shaking, didn't know what was going on.
We're on a brink of death.
And finally, water came out.
Because we didn't know what to expect-- or the gas, what they called the Zyklon gas at that time, or the water.
Luckily, the water came out.
Naturally, everybody was started to jump for joy, because we saw-- we went through.
And possible now we have a chance to survive.
After that was-- I remember like today-- was a very cold and rainy evening.
They started to drag us around the camp.
We had to run, like six in a row, all around the camp, for maybe 2 hours, naked, without any clothing whatsoever.
We came back again.
And they gave us-- each and everybody got slacks and a shirt or whatever.
And they ask us to go to a very big room, where 801 people had to sit and spend the night sitting.
But it wasn't space for 801 people.
So we had to spread our legs like eagles.
And each and every one was pushing in, pushing in, for the whole night.
That's how we survived the first five nights.
The fifth night, we heard somebody came in.
And they sending out the 801 men to Brunnlitz.
They packed us in again, in cattle wagons.
And Brunnlitz is the same thing-- in Sudetenland, not far, where Oskar Schindler was born.
He was born in the city called Zwittau.
And took us-- I don't remember how long.
But couldn't take long, because Gross-Rosen, from Zwittau or Brunnlitz, is not far.
Took us maybe a day.
When we arrived, on the platform, on the train station, on a side, was standing a tall man-- I saw him the first time in my life-- with the Tyrol, Tyrol hat with a feather.
It was Oskar Schindler with the SS people, who were transporting us to the camp of Brunnlitz.
This was the camp what we were working from, approximately the 21st-- when we arrived to Brunnlitz-- till the liberation day of 9 of May, 1945.
During those days, first of all, the food improved immediately, because everybody got a bigger ration.
We got coffee.
We got soap, what was really something what didn't exist in our [LAUGHS] language anymore.
One day Oskar Schindler came.
And this was just before the High Holidays.
Came to the working place.
And he ask everybody to close the doors from the outside, from our people.
And he had a speech to us.
He said, I know your holidays are coming up now.
And I will try to give you whatever possible, in-- in order to rejoice the holidays.
Something like that, many of us didn't hear for many years from a German, from a Nazi.
Because whatever it is, he was still a Nazi.
But the man was human.
His approach was completely different than anybody what we knew before.
We were working during the seven months for him, hoping one day, we'll survive.
Because each and every time he came up, he always had a few nice words, kind words, to each and every one of us.
And we felt very good to be under his protection.
There were a number of things what was going on during that time.
One of the men was trying to get [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rid of him was a top man of the SS men.
He was the commandant of the camp.
His name was Oberster Fuhrer Leopold.
Leopold was his name.
And he was trying to liquidate the whole camp, in order to shoot in every-- every one of us.
And when he-- when Oskar Schindler find out about his approach, he was trying to get rid of him.
And one day, they were drinking very heavy-- Oskar Schindler and this Commandant Leopold.
He gave him [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like a bomb, to throw on one of the garages there, what we didn't use.
That's what he did, he was drunk.
Immediately, he notified Berlin.
And they sent out this SS man from the camp, because he felt he could be danger to our survival.
He did the utmost, in order for us to survive.
In the beginning, was bad.
Because when we arrived, the camp wasn't ready to absorb so many people.
Now this was a factory, actually.
So the bunks weren't ready.
We had to sleep on concrete floors, where water was running on the floors.
But we didn't have any other alternative.
And we were happy to have this, but just to stay with Oskar Schindler, his camp.
But slowly, we were building up bunks.
And we started to move to the bunks.
But at the beginning was quite tough, because nothing was prepared yet.
He was trying, in a hurry, to get out all the Jews, the 1,100.
Because-- you know, 300 women, they were sent out to Auschwitz.
And they came-- he went to Auschwitz, in order to get them out, what he did successfully.
Many of the women there had husbands already in camp.
And that's how 1,100 Jews survived.
No.
In the beginning, yes.
But when Oskar Schindler went to Poland, he was arrested for wheeling and dealing and working on a black market.
Because Amon Goth was arrested the same time, when they found in his possession all kinds of cigarettes, cognacs, and I heard 8-- over 80,000 marks, which was quite a big amount of money.
This was the money and the cognac and everything what he was getting from Oskar Schindler, because he was bribing this commandant of Plaszow.
He was arrested, and Oskar Schindler was arrested.
When he wasn't around in Brunnlitz, Leopold-- the commandant of the camp-- sent back a number of children, back to Auschwitz.
By the way, they survive.
There were a few kids.
No.
I-- by sight, yes.
But personally, no.
Yeah, he was working in the office.
He was working in the office.
And he didn't come down together with us, because we are working in the factory.
And they were working in a different building.
In the office, there were a number of people, like Itzhak Stern, Marcel Goldberg.
Those were the two main people who prepared the famous list, Schindler's list.
There were SS surrounded in the camp.
But Schindler didn't allow them to enter the factory.
They were not allowed to enter the factory without his permission.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they knew he is very powerful, and he can do many things.
So nobody wanted to touch him.
How did that make you feel?
Very good, like everybody else.
The problem was always to keep out the SS, because they were vicious.
But even by then, where everybody else was already sympathetic, this group, the SS group, were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very, very dangerous to each and every one of us.
Yes.
We had a hospital.
We had a small hospital, where came to life Mrs.
Schindler.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was helping out very much, trying to get medication and what have you and helping out the people.
But this was naturally by the end.
Because she was-- during the time when he was in Poland, she didn't stay in Poland.
She was staying in their hometown, at Zwittau.
And she knew he is unfaithful to her.
Like I mentioned, he was a very heavy drinker, womanizer, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gambler, name it.
Twice.
[DRINKS] But naturally as a worker to a director of the camp.
There were only a number of people.
Because by then, we were disorganized completely.
People were scared, still terribly scared, because tomorrow was so unpredictable.
Till the last moment, we didn't believe we are going to survive.
Because they told her a number of time, whatever it is-- the German, the SS-- will come in and put us away, one by one.
You know, The way of thinking was completely different what we think today.
Because our way of thinking was only to survive.
But survive was not-- it wasn't a possibility by then.
Because so many people what we knew died around us.
Nobody died during Schindler's reign, luckily.
Yes, it happened in February-- January or February 1945.

When the Germans were already running from the Eastern Front and the Russian were moving them further and further inside Germany, a few wagons with Hungarian Jews were on the site not far from our camp.
And they left them over there to die.
Schindler got the knowledge of it.
Is a transport over there, there are a number of Jews on the side tracks, and they dying.
And the help is not going to come fast.
They will die over the night.
He took a number of men from our camp, took a few SS men, a few trucks.
And they opened the wagons.
The wagons were completely closed.
When they opened the wagons, many, many were dead completely.
Some of them, even alive, they were trying to pick them up, but they couldn't.
They were frozen to the steel or whatever.
And I don't know how many people.
But there were maybe 80 people what they brought to our camp.
And by then, Mrs.
Schindler put her good work.
And because of her, many survived.
She was helping them, bringing medication, trying to warm them up and what have you.
She brought-- many died.
But many survive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Emilie was a good person.
But she was always on the side.
She didn't have too much to say, as long Oskar Schindler was in front.
Who?
Yes, he did, yes, he did, a few times.
He said to her patient-- the-- even once, he said, the war is coming to end.
This was practically weeks before the liberation.
And he said, we have a chance to survive, and he will do the utmost, in order for us and him to survive.
Good or average.
Because like I said before, he was trying to bring as much food was possible.
He was buying-- buying it on black market.
Or when the Russian Army were pushing the German deeper, deeper into Germany, they left, let's say, wagons of all kinds of food.
He was bringing it in into the camp and distributing, naturally, between the-- the prisoners.
There were a number of bombings, not directly in our surrounding.
But we heard, many times, big explosions.
Oh, we knew was the Russian Army.
Because this part was closer to the Russian Front-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after Poland, Czechoslovakia, Sudetenland, and deeper into Germany.
That's correct, yeah.
He did.
He did, but very short trips.
No.
Only the few children what used to belong to the people who were very close with sponsoring the list.
Yes.
Yes, we knew.
But this was moments of hope and moments of depressions.
Because we were afraid, as before, they will come to our camp-- the Russians-- we are not going to be alive anymore.
Each and every minute, every second could mean dead.
Not directly.
No, because we didn't have any direct contact with the SS.
Like I mentioned before, Schindler told them not to enter the factory.
[DRINKS]
Yes, I saw the guards surrounded, on the towers, with the machine gun facing the camp.
We didn't have nothing to do-- no contact with those people.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
